coredata.dc:title: Sustainable synthesis of bright green fluorescent carbon quantum dots from lignin for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Lingli
Shen, Dekui
Liu, Qian
Wu, Chunfei
Gu, Sai
coredata.dc:description: Biomass transformation into high-value carbon nanomaterials plays a dual role of easing the energy material crisis and reasonable disposal of waste. Here, we employed biomass lignin as carbon source to synthesize fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through a facile two-step method. The preparation condition is optimized at 0.3 g of acid additive and 200 ℃, 16 h of hydrothermal treatment. The resultant CQDs with the average diameter of 4.86 nm and high quantum yield of 23.68% are composed of carbon cores and surface functional groups, exhibiting the maximum green emission wavelength of 476 nm. The formation mechanism involves acid dissociation of lignin in the first step, and then the aromatic refusion of lignin fragments. The CQDs show great advantages as fluorescent nanoprobe for metal ion detection, which present an apparent and selective fluorescence quenching effect on Fe3+. This CQD nanoprobe has a highly sensitive response to Fe3+ ions in the concentration of 0 ~ 300 μM with detection limit of 0.77 μM. The utilization of renewable biomass lignin in this study not only opens the avenue of the sustainable, cost-effective and mass production of CQDs, but also provides a novel alternative nanoprobe for sensing field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Lignin
Carbon quantum dots
Formation mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of preparation conditions on QYs
Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The relative QYs and images of the CQDs under irradiation with sunlight and UV light (365 nm) in different preparation conditions: (a) retention time (4–18 h), (b) hydrothermal temperature (140–240 ℃), (c) additive amount of o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid (0.1–1.1 g).
(a) TEM, HRTEM images, particle size distribution, FFT pattern, (b) XRD pattern and (c) Raman spectrum of the resultant CQDs.

(a) The UV–vis absorption, insert the photograph of the CQDs under sunlight (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (b) PL emission spectra of the CQDs under different excitation wavelengths, insert the PL emission spectra after normalization processing.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CQDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s (d) N 1 s, (e) S 2p of AL, LNP and CQD. (f) The speculated structure of CQDs.

Main substructures identified in the two-dimensional HSQC NMR spectra of (a) AL, (b) LNP and (c) CQD: (A) β-aryl-ether (β-O-4 linkages), (B) phenylcoumaran (β--5 linkages), (C) resinol (β–β linkages), (pCA) p-coumarate, (FA) ferulate, (H) p-hydroxyphenyl and (G) guaiacyl units.
The possible formation mechanism of lignin-derived CQDs via two-step route.

(a) CQDs solution under 365 nm UV light illumination. (b) The fluorescence response s of CQDs with the existence of various metal ions (0.1 M). (c) The PL emission spectra of CQDs treated by different Fe3+ concentrations (0–500 μM). (d) The variation curve of the 1-F0/F value with the concentrations of Fe3+, insert the linear fit at low concentrations of Fe3+ (0–300 μM).
The two-step synthetic process of lignin-derived CQDs.
Comparison of different fluorescent nanoprobes for Fe3+ detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MoS2 quantum dots, b graphene quantum dots, c carbon dots, d nano-biomass dots, e Au nanoclusters, f nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots, g nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots

head.ce:title: Sustainable synthesis of bright green fluorescent carbon quantum dots from lignin for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Energy Thermal Conversion and Control of Ministry of Education, School of Energy and Environment, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Energy Thermal Conversion and Control of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Queen’s University Belfast, Belfast BT7 1NN, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Queen’s University Belfast, Belfast BT7 1NN, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering and Physical Sciences, University of Surrey, Guilford GU2 7XH, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering and Physical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Engineering and Physical Sciences, University of Surrey, Guilford GU2 7XH, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass transformation into high-value carbon nanomaterials plays a dual role of easing the energy material crisis and reasonable disposal of waste. Here, we employed biomass lignin as carbon source to synthesize fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through a facile two-step method. The preparation condition is optimized at 0.3 g of acid additive and 200 ℃, 16 h of hydrothermal treatment. The resultant CQDs with the average diameter of 4.86 nm and high quantum yield of 23.68% are composed of carbon cores and surface functional groups, exhibiting the maximum green emission wavelength of 476 nm. The formation mechanism involves acid dissociation of lignin in the first step, and then the aromatic refusion of lignin fragments. The CQDs show great advantages as fluorescent nanoprobe for metal ion detection, which present an apparent and selective fluorescence quenching effect on Fe3+. This CQD nanoprobe has a highly sensitive response to Fe3+ ions in the concentration of 0 ~ 300 μM with detection limit of 0.77 μM. The utilization of renewable biomass lignin in this study not only opens the avenue of the sustainable, cost-effective and mass production of CQDs, but also provides a novel alternative nanoprobe for sensing field.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy shortage, gradual depletion of non-renewable resources coupled with the worsening of environment are global challenges that human is confronted with [1,2]. In this case, biomass conversion takes great potential to alleviate these issues due to its clean, abundant and renewable resources [3]. Several thermochemical techniques including pyrolysis, combustion and hydrothermal carbonization are employed to transform biomass waste into value-added products such as bio-oil, biochar, expensive chemicals [4]. Recently, the green synthesis of novel carbon materials from biomass have driven an explosive amount of research [5].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a rising star in carbon nanomaterial family have attracted worldwide attention since discovered in 2004 due to their excellent hydrophilcity, biocompatibility, high conductivity, low toxicity, and controllable fluorescent properties [6,7]. In view of low cost and environmental friendliness, a variety of biomass such as rice husk [8], lemon juice [9], watermelon peel [10], durian [11], coffee grounds [12], wool [13], prawn shell [14] have served to produce CQDs by bottom-up methods, which are widely applied in photocatalysis, bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, solar cell, adsorption, supercapacitor and electrode [15–19]. Lignin as one of biomass three components supplied by all types of agricultural and forestry plants, is an alternative and sustainable carbon precursor to produce CQDs. Zhang et al. [20] synthesized N-CQDs from lignin and ethylenediamine via an one-pot hydrothermal treatment at 180 ℃ for 12 h. Gao et al. [21] prepared blue CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of lignin at 180 ℃ for 12 h in a strong acid environment (H2SO4: HNO3 = 3:1). Meanwhile, Ding et al. [22] obtained N-doped graphene quantum dots from lignin by a novel two-step route, involving the oxidized cleavage step (HNO3, ultrasonicated for 12 h) and followed by the hydrothermal step (180 °C, 12 h). These CQDs prepared with the addictive of concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid. This method is not cost-effective and the corrosion of strong acid poses a threat to the environment and human health. The fluorescence quantum yields of the reported biomass-derived CQDs are less than 21% and limited to the emission of blue wavelengths, thus restricting their broad application. Heteroatom doping of N, S, P, etc. is confirmed to be an effective method to adjust the physicochemical properties of CQDs [23,24]. Although the heating temperature, retention time and additive amount have great effects on the QY of CQDs, it has rarely been systematically researched. The potential formation mechanism of CQDs from diverse biomass has not been thoroughly explored.

Fe3+ is an important trace element in biological systems as well as a common heavy metal contaminant of natural water environments. The accumulational Fe3+ levels (≥0.3 mg/L) in the living cell and water body would result in serious diseases and pollution. Thus, the sensitive detection of Fe3+ have a great significance [25,26]. Fluorescent nanoprobes based on nanoscale CQDs are superior to those traditional technologies such as ultraviolet spectroscopy, complexometric titration, and atomic absorption spectrometry, owing to their competitive detection limit, high reliability and faster response [27,28]. Two alternative mechanisms (the static quenching and dynamic quenching) are used to explain the fluorescence quenching effect, which are determined by the interactions between Fe3+ and the nanoprobe. Up to now, lots of CQD nanoprobes for the detection of Fe3+ have already been reported, it is still desirable to develop more efficient CQD nanoprobe with low cost, simple process and great practicability [29].

Herein, a facile and effective two-step route was proposed to synthesize CQDs from biomass lignin through mild additive of o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid. As compared with other chemical reagents such as concentrated sulfuric and nitride acid, the organic acid is much more environmentally friendly and less corrosive. The optimal reaction conditions and physicochemical properties of as-obtained CQDs were investigated. The formation mechanism of lignin-derived CQDs in the two-step route was explored through the comparative analysis of FT-IR, XPS, 2D-HSQC NMR results of raw materials, intermediates and target products. The application of the CQD nanoprobe in Fe3+ detection was evaluated. It hopes to not only seek a sustainable, green and low-cost route to produce CQDs in large scale, but also offer a new insight of biomass waste disposal and utilization.

Alkali lignin (AL) with low sulfonate content was purchased from Sigma in Shanghai (CAS: 8068–05-1). The o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, KCl, NiCl2·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, NaCl, CaCl2·6H2O, were provided by Aladdin in Shanghai. The dialysis bag of retained molecular weight of 3500 Da was supplied by Viskase (MD44, US). The mixed cellulose ester filter membranes (Φ = 25 mm) were obtained by Relab (0.8 and 0.22 μm, Shanghai).

In the two-step synthesis route of CQDs (Scheme 1 ), the o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid (0.1–1.1 g) was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized (DI) water, and then gradually added 0.3 g of AL under constant stirring. The mixed solution was heated in a thermostatic magnetic stirrer at 90 °C for 1 h at a stirring speed of 30 rpm. The solution was subsequently vacuum filtered by a 0.8 μm filter membrane. Afterwards the obtained filtrate was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and kept at 140–240 °C for 4–18 h under natural pressure. After cooling to room temperature, the insoluble carbon in the water-soluble CQDs solution was filtered out with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The brown supernatant was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48 h and freeze-dried until the sample powder was obtained for investigation and characterization. The intermediate reactant (lignin nanoparticles, LNPs) can be directly obtained by freeze-dried filtrate before hydrothermal treatment.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20) was conducted to determine the morphologies of lignin-derived CQDs. The ultraviolet − visible (UV–vis) absorption property of CQDs was measured by a UV-5200 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were characterized by Agilent Cary Eclipse at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku RU-200b) and Raman spectra (532 nm, ThermoFisher Dxi) were utilized to determine the crystalline structure of CODs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher ESCALAB 250Xi) were carried out to determine the surface chemical composition and chemical states of AL, LNP and CQD. The two-dimensional heteronuclear singular quantum correlation nuclear magnetic resonance (2D-HSQC NMR, Bruker 400 M) experiments were performed to obtain the structural connection of 1H and 13C of samples dispersed in D2O.

The relative quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs can be calculated by Eqs. (1) as follow:
(1)
φ
x
=
φ
std
I
x
I
std
A
std
A
x
η
x
η
std
2
where φx is the fluorescence QY, x represents the CQDs. std is the quinine sulfate, which was selected as the reference compound and dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (φstd = 0.54). η is the refractive index (1.33 for aqueous solution). A is the optical absorbance at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. I is the integrated fluorescence intensity under the fluorescence emission spectrum. To reduce the interference of the reabsorption effects, the absorbance at excitation 340 nm were controlled to 0.05.

To evaluate the selective detection on metal ions, the 2 mL CQDs solutions (0.125 mg/mL) were mixed with 20 μL metal ion solution (0.05 mol/L) of K+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Na+, Ca2+, respectively. The fluorescence responses were recorded after fully stirring and letting stand for 15 min at room temperature. The sensitive detection test was confirmed by adding different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–500 μM) to 2 mL CQDs solution. The fluorescence responses were also characterized by the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of preparation conditions on QYs
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that retention time, hydrothermal temperature and additive amount have significant effects on the preparation of CQDs. The QYs are measured at 340 nm excitation wavelength in order to evaluate the fluorescence performance of CQDs. As presented in Fig. 1 a, b, the synthesized CQDs solution becomes brighter under irradiation with ultraviolet light (UV) with the increase of heating time (4–16 h) and temperature (140–200 ℃), implying an elevated CQDs concentration. Afterwards, the color darkens at extended heating time (16–18 h) and high temperatures (200–240 ℃). These results are consistent with previous research and speculated that insufficient reaction occurs at short heating time and low temperature, while excessive carbonization at overtime and high temperature leads to the agglomeration of CQDs [30–32]. The QY is raised to 23.68% upon increasing the acid amount from 0.1 to 0.3 g, while the continuous increased additive amount from 0.3 to 1.1 g do not further improve the QYs (Fig. 1c). This may be due to the limited reaction space in 30 mL DI. The lignin-derived CQDs prepared without any acid addictive exhibit almost non-fluorescence and its QY is negligible. This is probably due to the water insolubility and good chemical stability of alkali lignin under mild hydrothermal treatment. Therefore, the optimal preparation condition of CQDs is at the hydrothermal temperature of 200 ℃, retention time of 16 h and acid amount of 0.3 g, the morphology, structure and optical properties of which is selected to be further characterized and investigated.

As distinctly shown in the TEM image (Fig. 2 a), the as-prepared CQDs are zero-dimensional and discrete quasi-spherical nanoparticles with the average diameter of 4.86 ± 1.44 nm. The amplified atom-resolution structure can be observed by HR-TEM image, resembling graphitic (0 0 2) crystal plane with a lattice spacing of ca. 0.314 nm, similar to the previous studies of biomass-derived CQDs [14,33–35]. The bright points result from the diffraction of (0 0 2) and (0 0 2) planes are observed in the fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern, which are identical to the high quality graphitic crystals. XRD patterns and Raman spectra were performed to investigate the crystalline structure of CQDs. As depicted in Fig. 2b, a typical (0 0 2) diffraction peak of CQDs is at 2θ = 26.6°. The interlayer spacing of 3.36 Å is associated with that of bulk graphite. Compared with the broad peak of AL (2θ = 20.7°), the peak of CQDs is shifted and narrowed attributing to the improved crystal degree from amorphous AL to high crystalline CQDs [36–38]. The graphitization degree of CQDs was recorded by the Raman spectrum (Fig. 2c). The D peak at 1346 cm−1 is associated with the vibrations of the sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, whereas the G peak at 1576 cm−1 is corresponding to the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the disordered D peak and ordered G peak is around 0.78, indicative of the defect density in the CQDs, similar to the systematic structure of graphite. The 2D peak of CQDs is observed at 2879 cm−1, corresponding to few layer graphene [22,39,40].

The optical properties of the as-prepared CQDs were characterized. As seen in Fig. 3 a, the CQDs aqueous solution exhibit bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs displays two prominent excitonic adsorption bands located around 255 nm and 354 nm and the optical adsorption edge is about 460 nm. The significant adsorption peak below 300 nm is attributed to the π–π* transition within the core of CQDs, implying the formation of conjugated skeletons [29,41]. The weak adsorption shoulder is assigned to the heteroatom-doped surface state or the energy trap caused by n-π* transition of functional groups [22,32,42]. The PL spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. 3b. The maximum PL emission peak of CQDs is centered at 476 nm under 340 nm excitation wavelength. The corresponding fluorescence quantum yield is approximately ca. 23.68%, which is superior to most previous biomass-derived CQDs [43–45]. Additionally, the emission wavelength is slowly red-shifted accompanied by decreasing fluorescence intensity as the increasing excitation wavelength from 280 to 460 nm with a 20 nm increment. This excitation-dependent behavior might be explained by the difference in the particle size and emissive trap sites on each CQD. The defects on the CQDs introduced by the oxygen and heteroatom functional groups acting as excitation energy traps result in the different PL properties [46–48].

The evolution of surface functional groups and elemental composition from AL, LNP to CQD was identified by FT-IR and XPS spectra. As illustrated by the FT-IR spectrum of AL in Fig. 4 a, the broad absorption band centered at 3585 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of –OH. The absorption bands around 2940, 1277, and 1025 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H, C-O and C-O-C bonds, respectively. As for that of LNP and CQD, the absorption bands inherit some natural features of AL, including the –OH and C–O bonds with the stretching vibration around 3418 and 1235 cm−1. The emerging peaks of CQD at 3332 cm−1, 1482 cm−1 and 746 cm−1 are corresponding to the bending vibration of N-H, C–N and C-S. Meanwhile, the absorption bound observed at 1628 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of aromatic C = C. Notably, the stretching vibration ascribing to the epoxide/ether C–O–C around 1025 cm−1 weakens from AL to LNP and CQD [22,41]. These results of FT-IR spectra are in accordance with the information of XPS spectra. The high resolution C1s spectra of AL, LNP and CQD (Fig. 4b) display similar deconvolution peaks at 283.2, 283.7 eV (C-C/C = C) and 285.0 eV (C-O) whereas the C-O-C at 286.8 eV disappears from AL to CQD, indicating the cleavage of ether linkage. It is further confirmed by the O1s spectrum of LNP and CQD (Fig. 4c), which displays a sharp decreased C-OH/C-O-C at 531.9 eV compared with that of AL. Particularly, the new peak emerges at 285.6 eV in the C1s spectra of LNP and CQD, which originates from C-S/C-N groups introduced by o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid. Meanwhile, the N1s spectrum can be fitted into three types of N atoms within the structure of CQD (but not in AL), namely, pyrrolic-N (397.8 or 398.4 eV), amine-N (400.5 eV) and graphitic-N (401.3 eV) (Fig. 4d) [14,49]. The S2p spectra of LNP and CQD show distinct increased peaks at 162.2, 166.8, 168.0 and 168.8 eV, which represent the thiophene S and –C–SOX– (x = 2, 3, 4) units, respectively (Fig. 4e). Based on the experimental evidences, it is reasonable to deduce that the ether linkages are cleaved during the transition from AL into CQD and the heteroatoms (N, S) are effectively doped [22,50]. The possible structure of CQDs is speculated in Fig. 4f. The synthesized CQDs might be composed of graphitic core with multiple functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2 and -SOX) grafted on the framework of carbon, resulting in excellent hydrophilicity and dispersibility in water for subsequent application in sensing [51–53].

In order to obtain further structural insights about the formation of lignin-derived CQDs, the two-dimensional HSQC NMR spectra of AL, LNP and CQD were characterized and is exhibited Fig. 5 . As is well-known, AL is an amorphous polymer with a three-dimensional network structure and is formed by connecting three types of phenylpropane units through ether bonds or C-C bonds. The 2D-HSQC NNR spectra of AL in Fig. 5a show apparent signals in the side-chain region (δC/δH: 50–75/3.2–4.2 ppm), which are attributed to the native structure of lignin including methoxyls (–OCH3), β-aryl-ether linkages (A), phenylcoumarane structures (B) and resinol linkages (C). In the HSQC data of LNP (Fig. 5b), the signals of A, B and C compounds completely vanish, implying the intensive dissociation of ether linkages and some C-C bonds under the mild condition of acid treatment. However, the signals of p-coumarate (pCA), ferulate (FA), p-hydroxyphenyl (H) and (G) guaiacyl units are clearly observed in the aromatic region (δC/δH: 118–134/6.8–7.8 ppm) [54]. In addition, the N, S doping evidenced by FT-IR and XPS results may contribute to abundant active sites exposed on the surface of LNP. After further hydrothermal reaction, no obvious difference can be seen between the HSQC data of CQD (Fig. 5c) and LNP, where the sp2 carbons of LNP are properly aromatically refused by the condensation of side chains.

From above analysis results, the possible formation mechanism of lignin-derived CQDs in this study involves acid dissociation of AL firstly, and then the aromatic refusion of LNP. (Fig. 6 ) In detail, the reactions of AL with o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid generate lignin fragments through breaking ether linkages and some C-C bonds, along with N, S co-doping. Accordingly, it can form several new types of active sites including oxygen-, nitrogen- and sulfur- functional groups. Therefore, the o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid in this step plays a triple role of “top-down” scissors, oxidizers as well as dopants [22,41]. These intermediate molecules after being functionalized serve as carbon precursors for the synthesis of CQDs. Eventually, LNPs are aromatically refused via the prompt π-π stacking and the increased sp2 hybridization under the hydrothermal treatment. The C–N, C–S and C–SOx groups are simultaneously enhanced in the carbonization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In term of the facile synthetic route, low toxicity and superior optical properties, CQDs show promising application in various fields including sensor development. The selectivity of CQDs toward different metal ions was studied by examining the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) in the presence of K+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Na+ or Ca2+. As observed in Fig. 7 a, b, Fe3+ could quench 90% of the initial fluorescence intensity of CQDs while other metal ions show no distinct variation to fluorescence. As the previous literature reported, Liu et al. [55,56] explained the high selectivity might be caused by the faster chelating process of Fe3+ ions with CQDs compared to other ions. Deng et al. [57] proposed that the coordination complexes originate from the oxygen-containing functional groups of CQDs and Fe3+ ions resulted in the fluorescence quenching. Qi et al. [58] noted that the fluorescence quenching was static quenching owing to no change of the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs with and without Fe3+ ions. To our knowledge, Fe3+ ions with open d orbitals render the chelation appearance in the presence of electron donors. As for our prepared CQDs, the abundant oxygen functional groups (particularly carboxyl, hydroxyl groups) not only are electron donors but also crosslink ions to generate non-fluorescence complexes on the edge of CQDs. Thus, the fluorescence quenching may due to the electronic transition from excited state of CQDs to the d-orbital of Fe3+ ions [59,60]. Among above ions Fe2+ exerted little or no influence on the quenching of the PL intensity. Fe2+ has one more electron than Fe3+ in its outermost d orbital and the former is reductive while the latter is oxidizing. It can be speculated that Fe3+ would be reduced to Fe2+ in aqueous solution with the existence of CQDs, leading to the fluorescence quenching.

To assess the sensitivity of the CQDs for Fe3+ detection, varying concentrations from 0 to 500 μM of Fe3+ were added to the CQDs aqueous suspension, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. 7c, the PL intensities of resulting mixtures correspondingly drop as the increasing Fe3+ concentration, suggesting the high sensitivity of the concentration dependent fluorescence quenching. As displayed in Fig. 7d, the variation curve of the 1-F0/F value (F0 and F represent the PL intensities with and without metal ions, respectively) with the concentrations of Fe3+ fits a good linear below the concentration of 300 μM and the correlation coefficient square (R2) is 0.99373. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as low as 0.77 μM (3δ/m, δ and m represent the standard deviation of the blank signal and the slope of the linear fitting line, respectively). The LOD of the as-prepared CQD nanoprobe is superior in Fe3+ detection, which is much lower than both those reported by the previous literature (Table 1 ) and advocated by World Health Organization (5.36 μM) [67]. It is worth noting that although the CQD nanoprobe has not exceed expensive Au nanoclusters in the detection range of Fe3+, it shows a great potential in the Fe3+ detection application owing to its high sensitivity, low cost, sustainability and environmental friendliness.

The green synthesis of high-quality CQDs was proposed through a facile two-step route from renewable, low-cost, and environmentally friendly biomass lignin instead of toxic organic precursors. The fluorescence QY of CQDs is optimized as high as 23.68%, which is superior to most previous CQDs derived from biomass waste. The resultant CQDs with an average size of 4.86 ± 1.44 nm are composed of graphitic core with multiple functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2 and -SOX) with excitation-dependent wavelength and excellent water-solubility. The formation mechanism is clarified that ether linkages and some C-C bonds of AL are cleaved in the first acidolysis step, simultaneous N, S co-doped in the lignin fragments, followed by aromatic refusion via the prompt π-π stacking to generate CQDs. The CQDs are effectively used as fluorescent nanoprobe with high sensitivity and high selectivity for detecting Fe3+. Our work offers a sustainable strategy for biomass waste transition into high-value carbon nanomaterials, opening the new avenue of Fe3+ detection application.

Lingli Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Dekui Shen: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Qian Liu: Supervision, Validation, Investigation. Chunfei Wu: Supervision, Project administration. Sai Gu: Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51676047 and 51861145102) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Graduate School of Southeast University, China (YBPY2109).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on the enhancement of figure of merit from bulk to nano-thermoelectric materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Alam, S. Ramakrishna, A review on the enhancement of figure of merit from bulk to nano-thermoelectric materials, Nano Energy 2 (2013) 190-212.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Urbanization, energy consumption and emissions in the indian context a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Franco, V. R. Mandla, K. Ram Mohan Rao, Urbanization, energy consumption and emissions in the indian context a review, Renewable Sustainable Energy Rev. 71 (2017) 898-907.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering carbon materials from the hydrothermal carbonization process of biomass

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Hu, K. Wang, L. Wu, S. H. Yu, M. Antonietti, M. M. Titirici, Engineering carbon materials from the hydrothermal carbonization process of biomass, Adv. Mater. 22 (2010) 813-828.
